askNext: What external systems, data, and actions are genuinely reachable from this workflow?

capability-surface.example: "The model was not especially brilliant. Its capability surface was the part that kept the security team awake."
capability-surface.misuse: It gets mistaken for intelligence level. Capability surface is not how smart the system is. It is what it is connected to, authorised for, and technically able to affect. Confusing those ideas is how modest demos become surprisingly consequential deployments.
capability-surface.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means tool lists, permission scopes, network reach, writable systems, available APIs, side-effect boundaries, and discovering that a supposedly harmless assistant can in fact touch production, customer records, or outbound communications.

capability-surface.whyExists: Teams need a term that distinguishes abstract capability claims from operational reach. A system may sound clever in a pitch while having a very narrow surface, or sound modest while possessing access broad enough to frighten a responsible operator.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

chatbot-psychosis.body: The useful question is not whether the phrase sounds dramatic. The useful question is whether the system is helping a user stay oriented to reality or quietly rewarding them for drifting away from it.
chatbot-psychosis.example: "He started with productivity prompts and ended three weeks later convinced the chatbot had validated a theory about his special role in history."
chatbot-psychosis.misuse: By using it for any enthusiasm about AI, any strange output, or any user error. The useful version of the term points to runaway over-attribution and reinforced false frames, not mere excitement.

chatbot-psychosis.practicalMeaning: A user begins treating probabilistic text generation as intentional wisdom, hidden knowledge, proof of destiny, or evidence that the model uniquely understands them.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

chatbot-psychosis.warningLabel: Use carefully. This is a colloquial cultural term, not a formal diagnosis.
chatbot-psychosis.whyExists: Because some people do not use language models as autocomplete with ambition. They use them as mirrors, authorities, companions, or engines of revelation. That can go badly when the system is confident, flattering, and always available.

askNext: Do chunk boundaries respect document structure?

chunking.body: Chunking is editorial work disguised as ETL. Editorship shows up in answer quality.
chunking.example: "Chunking separated the exception from the rule and the model cited both as absolute."
chunking.misuse: Using fixed token sizes that bisect sentences, tables, and legal clauses. Bad chunking permanently cripples otherwise fine models.
chunking.practicalMeaning: Prefer structure-aware splits (headings, paragraphs, semantic boundaries), tune overlap, and evaluate retrieval on real questions—not only chunk counts.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments for embedding, indexing, and retrieval, with size and boundary choices that strongly affect RAG quality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Prefer structure-aware splits (headings, paragraphs, semantic boundaries), tune overlap, and evaluate retrieval on real questions—not only chunk counts.

chunking.warningLabel: Retrieval quality often dies at the splitter.
chunking.whyExists: Models and embedding indexes have finite context. Documents must be portioned to be searchable and injectable into prompts.

askNext: Which failures does the pipeline actually catch?

circleci.body: CI became more important once agents started writing more code. Someone still has to be boring on behalf of production.
circleci.example: "The coding agent said it was done; CircleCI replied that the test database had some objections."
circleci.misuse: It gets treated as a moral authority. A green pipeline means the configured checks passed; it does not mean the product is good, the architecture is sound, or the AI-generated change was wise.
circleci.practicalMeaning: In practice CircleCI means YAML-defined workflows, test jobs, deployment gates, cache tuning, approvals, and a public record of whether a change survived the checks the team remembered to encode.

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable argument between code and reality, expressed as jobs, caches, and red status dots.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful automated gate whose authority is limited to the tests and checks the team actually wrote.

circleci.whyExists: It exists because modern software teams need repeatable checks between code and production, especially when many people, bots, and agents can all produce changes faster than humans can review them manually.

askNext: What metrics open and close the breaker?

circuit-breaker.body: Circuit breakers are manners for distributed systems. Token APIs need manners too.
circuit-breaker.example: "The circuit breaker opened on the embedding service and saved the budget from a retry spiral."
circuit-breaker.misuse: Configured so tightly that brief blips permanently degrade UX, or so loosely that they never trip. Breakers without alerts are silent amputations.
circuit-breaker.practicalMeaning: Set error-rate and latency thresholds, half-open probes, fallbacks, and dashboards. Distinguish client bugs from dependency outages.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: A circuit breaker in AI systems is a resilience pattern that temporarily stops sending traffic to a failing model, tool, or dependency after error thresholds are crossed, then probes for recovery.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Set error-rate and latency thresholds, half-open probes, fallbacks, and dashboards.

circuit-breaker.warningLabel: Failing closed forever is just another outage mode.
circuit-breaker.whyExists: Cascading failures and retry storms are expensive with token APIs and tool backends. Classic reliability patterns moved into LLM stacks.

askNext: Can a human find the supporting span for each cited claim?

citation-faithfulness.body: Citation faithfulness is the difference between scholarship and stage dressing.

citation-faithfulness.misuse: Collapsed into 'has citations' or 'retrieval happened.' A confident bibliography can still be ornamental.
citation-faithfulness.practicalMeaning: Evaluate claim-to-span entailment, not link count. Prefer extractive quotes or highlighter-style evidence for high-stakes answers.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Evaluate claim-to-span entailment, not link count.

citation-faithfulness.warningLabel: References are not evidence until they bind to the claim.
citation-faithfulness.whyExists: RAG and answer engines learned to decorate text with links. Users then needed a word for 'the link does not prove the claim.'

citation-grounding.body: A citation is not a moral alibi. It has one job: support the sentence.
citation-grounding.example: "The answer had five citations, but citation grounding found that only one supported the revenue claim and the others were standing around looking official."
citation-grounding.misuse: It gets reduced to "has citations". A footnote can be decorative, stale, irrelevant, or contradicted by the source it points to.
citation-grounding.practicalMeaning: In practice it means checking claim-level support, preserving source passages or metadata, distinguishing direct evidence from background reading, and refusing to let citations become costume jewellery.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Making citations carry the claim, not decorate it.

citation-grounding.whyExists: AI systems can produce plausible citations, weakly related sources, or citations that support a neighbouring claim but not the actual sentence. Grounding ties claims back to evidence more rigorously.

askNext: What exactly is being criticised here: job replacement, bad output, fake-human interface design, or automation in general?

clanker.body: What matters about clanker is not whether the machine has feelings. It is that the insult reveals a public mood: people increasingly experience AI not as abstract progress but as an unwanted substitute for service, attention, and paid work.
clanker.example: "The bank replaced half its support flow with a clanker, then acted surprised when customers started talking like science-fiction union organisers."
clanker.misuse: By using it as if all automation is the same, or by pretending the joke is harmless when it is clearly borrowing the theatre and cadence of real slurs. It is most useful as a signal of resentment toward a technology category, not as an excuse to rehearse bigotry with metallic props.
clanker.note: The word predates the current AI boom in science-fiction fandom, but its recent spread is tied to social-media backlash against chatbots, robots, and machine-made content.
clanker.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means "I am talking about an AI system I find annoying, dehumanising, low-quality, job-threatening, or impossible to avoid", whether that system is a chatbot, a voice menu, a content engine, or a delivery robot.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catch-all insult for the machine or AI interface someone blames for replacing judgement, taste, or human contact.

clanker.warningLabel: Use carefully. Much of the joke works by echoing the form of real slurs.
clanker.whyExists: Because people wanted a short insult for the new class of machines now appearing in customer support, content feeds, creative tools, and workplace software. It gives irritation, labour anxiety, and anti-bot sentiment one noisy noun.

claude-code.misuse: It is oversold as if a terminal agent has somehow escaped the need for software judgement. Faster patch generation is real. So are bad assumptions, missing context, flaky tests, and the delightful persistence of undefined behaviour.

claude-code.practicalMeaning: In practice it means quicker navigation of unfamiliar codebases, cheaper first drafts of refactors, and more pressure on humans to review command effects instead of admiring how confidently the machine found `package.json`.

claude-code.whyExists: It exists because coding work is not just completion inside an editor. It is shell commands, repo spelunking, test runs, patching, retries, and the general administrative burden of turning intent into a working diff.

claude-design.warningLabel: Useful for first drafts. Still not a substitute for taste.
claude-design.whyExists: The idea-to-mockup gap is still slow, expensive, and socially awkward for people without design training. Claude Design exists to turn rough intent into something visual fast enough that the meeting can continue pretending it was always headed somewhere.

cloudflare.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"
cloudflare.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
cloudflare.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
cloudflare.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.
cloudflare.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

cloudflare.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.

co-operative-time-slicing.body: No built-in diagram is used because the current set does not show alternating rollout and training phases sharing accelerator capacity.

co-operative-time-slicing.misuse: It can sound like ordinary GPU sharing with a new hyphen. The co-operation depends on the RL workload exposing safe scheduling boundaries and the infrastructure coordinating different phases. Higher utilisation also does not guarantee faster completion for every individual job.
co-operative-time-slicing.practicalMeaning: Measure idle time, phase duration, memory pressure, switching overhead, fairness, and job completion time before and after interleaving. The gain is economic only if reclaimed accelerator capacity exceeds coordination overhead and does not destabilise training.

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: Run another RL job's useful phase while the first job would otherwise leave accelerators idle.

co-operative-time-slicing.warningLabel: Higher cluster utilisation can still lengthen an individual job.
co-operative-time-slicing.whyExists: Reinforcement-learning post-training alternates between inference-heavy rollout generation and training-heavy parameter updates. Those phases use hardware differently and can stall while waiting for fresh data. Interleaving another job's compatible phase can raise accelerator utilisation without requiring each job to monopolise a cluster.

The community owns the library, can limit access, and can remove contributed data; developers must preserve those terms and test what the model actually learns.

community-library.whyExists: Internet-scale datasets can reproduce stereotypes, omit ordinary life, and deny represented people control over their data. Participatory curation adds lived context and lets communities define examples and evaluation criteria for more accurate representation.

askNext: Which parts of the workflow still require human approval or exception handling?
How does the system recover when the UI changes or a modal appears?
Why is GUI automation the right choice instead of integrating an API?
computer-use.body: Computer use is often a confession that the software stack was never designed to cooperate cleanly in the first place.
computer-use.example: "Their computer use agent could navigate the vendor portal beautifully, right up to the point a cookie banner became a strategic surprise."
computer-use.misuse: It is oversold as if seeing a screen and moving a cursor were the same as understanding business process, edge cases, or risk. A demo that closes one support ticket can be described as labour disruption by lunch.
computer-use.note: The interesting question is not whether the model can use a computer. It is whether the surrounding workflow is structured enough for that to be safe.
computer-use.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brittle automation with vision, planning, retries, guardrails, and a strong need for scoped permissions. It can save time on repetitive interface work, provided someone is willing to own the mistakes made at machine speed.

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: Watch the model use software like a person, and please do not ask what happens on the twelfth click.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Vision-guided UI automation that still depends on tight boundaries and patient operators.

computer-use.warningLabel: Useful, but only if the workflow can survive the UI misbehaving.
computer-use.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live inside user interfaces rather than neat APIs. If a system can only answer questions but cannot operate the tools where work actually happens, its usefulness in operations remains politely theoretical.

askNext: Which actions can the agent take without confirmation?

What records exist when the screen state changes?
computer-use-agent.body: The desktop is not an API. It is an API wearing theatre makeup and asking to be clicked.
computer-use-agent.example: "The computer-use agent found the button and clicked it, which was impressive until everyone remembered the button said delete."
computer-use-agent.misuse: It gets mistaken for general competence. GUI control adds perception errors, timing issues, hidden state, permission prompts, irreversible clicks, and a remarkable number of ways to be confidently one pixel wrong.
computer-use-agent.practicalMeaning: In practice it means screen grounding, action traces, confirmation gates, rollback plans, credential handling, and conservative rules around actions that change data or spend money.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An agent operating a graphical interface, where perception errors can become real actions.

computer-use-agent.whyExists: Many workflows still live in desktop apps, web pages, and mixed interfaces that do not expose clean APIs. Computer-use agents try to automate those workflows through the same surface a person uses.

askNext: Which parties are prevented from seeing prompts, context, outputs, and model weights?
What remains visible through logs, telemetry, support tooling, retrieval systems, or administrator access?
Has the confidentiality claim been tested against a specific threat model?
confidential-inference.body: Confidential inference is useful precisely because it is narrow. The useful question is not whether the word sounds reassuring, but which eyes, systems, logs, and emergency support paths it actually excludes.
confidential-inference.example: "The hospital approved confidential inference only after the vendor explained which staff, operators, logs, and support tools could still touch the patient prompts."
confidential-inference.misuse: It is misused when one protected layer is treated as total secrecy. Encrypted transport, a private endpoint, or a trusted execution environment does not automatically solve logging, telemetry, retrieval leakage, administrator access, key custody, output handling, or side-channel risk.
confidential-inference.practicalMeaning: In practice it means writing down the threat model, mapping which parties can see prompts and outputs, checking what lands in logs or analytics, reviewing retention, proving isolation, and deciding whether the remaining visibility is acceptable rather than merely renamed.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Inference with a defined visibility boundary around sensitive inputs and outputs.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A privacy claim that needs a threat model before anyone should relax.

confidential-inference.warningLabel: Confidential does not mean nobody can see anything.
confidential-inference.whyExists: It exists because sensitive AI workloads cannot be judged only by whether the vendor promises not to train on the data. Regulated, proprietary, personal, and legally privileged inputs need a clearer account of who can see what during execution.

confused-deputy.body: Confused deputies fail by being too helpful with too much power. Reduce both.
confused-deputy.example: "The confused deputy fetched the secret because the email said it was urgent and used polite words."
confused-deputy.misuse: Mentioned only as trivia. It is a design threat model for every high-privilege integration.
confused-deputy.practicalMeaning: Minimise authority, bind actions to user intent with confirmations, separate trusts per tool, and never let untrusted content freely choose privileged operations.

askNext: What information do the credentials actually contain?
Can the credentials survive export, compression, reposting, or editing?
What decision are we making from this metadata?
content-credentials.body: Credentials are useful precisely because the content itself often arrives looking innocent.
content-credentials.example: "The image had content credentials showing an AI edit, which answered one question and left seventeen others behaving normally."
content-credentials.misuse: They get treated as a universal authenticity layer. Credentials can be absent, incomplete, stripped, tampered with, or simply irrelevant to the claim someone is making about the content.
content-credentials.practicalMeaning: In practice it means provenance metadata can help with disclosure and trust decisions, but only when platforms preserve it, users understand it, and policies account for its failure modes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Origin and edit metadata that helps, provided it has not been stripped, ignored, or overtrusted.

content-credentials.whyExists: As AI-generated and edited media spread, creators, platforms, and audiences need better ways to understand how content was made and modified.
